title: Arabidopsis Leaf Protoplasting Protocol
author: Diep R R Ganguly
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.p9adr2e

steps.title: Enzyme Solution
steps.instruction: Enzyme Solution: Make solution of 20mM MES (pH 5.7), 0.4M Mannitol and 20mM KCl. Heat solution to 70°C for 3-5min. To this add cellulose R10 (1.5%) and macerozyme R10 (0.4%). Warm solution to 55°C for 10 min (inactivates DNAse and proteases and aids solubilising enzyme). Cool to room temperature and add 10mM Ca Cl 2 , 1-5mM ß-mercaptoethanol (optional) and 0.1% BSA. Filter the final enzyme solution through a 0.45um syringe filter into a petri dish (enzyme solution should be made fresh, however, can be stored overnight at 4°C). W5 Solution: 2mM MES (pH 5.7), 154mM NaCl, 125mM CaCl 2 and 5mM KCl. MMG Solution: 4mM MES (pH5.7), 0.4M Mannitol and 15mM MgCl 2 .

steps.title: Before starting experiment, it is considered...
steps.instruction: Before starting experiment, it is considered ideal to keep intended Arabidopsis plants for protoplast extraction in the dark overnight. This is so that all the starch is used up, making the isolation procedure easier (less junk in extract). Use 3-4 week old Arabidopsis plants (before flowering). Choose well-expanded leave (usually #5 & #7). Approx. 40 leaves (in 50ml enzyme solution) should give a yield of roughly 1, 500, 000 protoplasts (can extract almost 3ug RNA from this using TRIzol method). This is suitable for routine experiments. For larger scale: Sheen et al. suggests 100 – 150 leaves in 40 – 60ml enzyme solution yielding 10 7 .

steps.title: Cut 0
steps.instruction: Cut 0.5-1mm leaf strips of the leaf using a fresh sharp razor blade (on a nice cutting surface) without tissue crushing at the cutting site (i.e. try not to push down on the tissue with the blade, rather try to “glide” the blade to make the cut).

steps.title: Transfer leaf cuts quickly and gently...
steps.instruction: Transfer leaf cuts quickly and gently into enzyme solution and make sure leaf strips are submerged nicely.

steps.title: Continue digestion, without shaking, in the...
steps.instruction: Continue digestion, without shaking, in the dark for at least 3 hours @ room temperature (enzyme solution should turn from a light brown colour to green after gentle swirling which indicates protoplast release).

steps.title: Check for release of protoplasts in...
steps.instruction: Check for release of protoplasts in solution under the microscope (after gentle swirling). Arabidopsis mesophyll protoplasts are approximately 30 - 50 µm in diameter.

steps.title: Dilute the enzyme/protoplast solution with an...
steps.instruction: Dilute the enzyme/protoplast solution with an equal volume of W5 solution .

steps.title: Rinse 2 clean layers of miracloth in W5
steps.instruction: Rinse 2 clean layers of miracloth in W5. Filter the enzyme solution containing the protoplasts with these layers of miracloth.

steps.title: Centrifuge the flow-through at 150 g/rcf...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the flow-through at 150 g/rcf for 2 minutes (brake ON/hard stop) in a 30ml-round-bottom glass centrifuge tube. Remove as much supernatant as possible and re-suspend protoplasts by gentle swirling.

steps.title: Use haemocytometer to calculate the concentration of protoplasts
steps.instruction: Use haemocytometer to calculate the concentration of protoplasts.

steps.title: Re-suspend in W5 solution to a...
steps.instruction: Re-suspend in W5 solution to a concentration of 2x10 5 /mL.

steps.title: Rest protoplasts on ice for at least 30 minutes
steps.instruction: Rest protoplasts on ice for at least 30 minutes. Protoplasts should begin to settle to the bottom of the tube by gravity. Remove as much W5 solution as possible without touching the pellet. Re-suspend at 2x10 5 /mL in MMG solution kept at room temperature (will need to do another count using haemocytometer before resuspension).
